attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with two weeks of increasing fatigue, joint pain, and intermittent fever. She has a history of systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed three years ago. Her medications include hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone. She has no history of intravenous drug use.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. She has a new systolic murmur at the apex. There are no peripheral stigmata of infective endocarditis. Blood tests reveal haemoglobin 95 g/L (115-165), white cell count 3.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0), platelets 110 x 10^9/L (150-400), and elevated anti-double stranded DNA antibodies. Three sets of blood cultures show no growth after five days. Transoesophageal echocardiogram shows small vegetations on the mitral valve with mild regurgitation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of non-bacterial endocarditis in the setting of active systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis is the most likely diagnosis, supported by the presence of active SLE (evidenced by elevated anti-dsDNA antibodies), valve vegetations on echocardiography, and repeatedly negative blood cultures.

Infective endocarditis is less likely given the repeatedly negative blood cultures, absence of peripheral stigmata of endocarditis, and the presence of active SLE. While infection should always be considered, the clinical picture is more consistent with an immune-mediated process.

Rheumatic heart disease typically occurs following streptococcal pharyngitis and presents with different valve abnormalities. The acute phase would show carditis rather than vegetations, and chronic changes would show valve stenosis rather than small vegetations.

Rheumatic fever is an acute inflammatory condition following group A streptococcal infection, characterised by carditis, arthritis, chorea, and erythema marginatum. This patient has no history of recent streptococcal infection and the presentation is more consistent with active SLE.

Antiphospholipid syndrome can occur in SLE patients and can cause valve lesions, but these typically manifest as valve thickening rather than vegetations. While this patient may have concurrent antiphospholipid syndrome, the vegetations are more characteristic of nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis.

Nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis is characterised by sterile vegetations that typically affect the mitral valve, followed by the aortic valve. The diagnosis is supported by evidence of active SLE, valve vegetations on echocardiography, and the absence of infection on blood cultures.
learningPoints: Nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE) is characterised by sterile vegetations on cardiac valves in patients with systemic autoimmune conditions.
Negative blood cultures with valve vegetations in a patient with active SLE suggests non-infectious endocarditis.
Transoesophageal echocardiography is more sensitive than transthoracic echocardiography for detecting valve vegetations.
references: Quintero-Martinez JA, Hindy JR, El Zein S, et al. Contemporary demographics, diagnostics and outcomes in non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis. Heart. Published online May 9, 2022. doi:10.1136/heartjnl-2022-320970
